question: What's the cheapest time to fly to Orlando?
answer: January–February (post-holidays) and September–early November are the lowest-demand periods for both flights and park prices. Orlando International (MCO) is one of the most competitive air markets in Florida — Southwest, Frontier, and Spirit frequently run sub-$100 one-way fares from many US cities.

question: Which Orlando airport should I use?
answer: Orlando International (MCO) is the main airport, 20 minutes from Disney World via the 417 toll road. Sanford Airport (SFB) is 45 minutes north and served by a few low-cost carriers — useful only if the fare difference is significant. MCO has the best car rental options and is closest to most attractions.

question: Do I need a rental car in Orlando?
answer: Almost certainly yes if you're visiting multiple theme parks. Disney's Magical Express no longer runs, so you'll need a car or paid shuttle from the airport. Uber/Lyft works but adds up quickly across a week. If staying exclusively on Disney property and visiting only Disney parks, the free Disney bus network can substitute.

question: How far in advance should I book Orlando theme park tickets?
answer: Book 4–6 weeks ahead for Disney and Universal — both sell out peak dates for park passes. Disney requires both a ticket and a timed park reservation. Universal is more flexible but Express Passes sell out quickly. SeaWorld and LEGOLAND rarely sell out and often offer last-minute discounts.

question: Where should I stay in Orlando to save money?
answer: Off-Disney-property hotels on US-192 near Kissimmee cost 50–70% less than Disney resorts. Good chain options include Marriott and Hilton Garden Inn. If staying off-property near Universal, look for I-Drive hotels — many offer free shuttle service to the park gates.

question: What hidden costs should I plan for in Orlando?
answer: Budget for: park parking ($30–$45/day at Disney/Universal), Disney's Genie+ and Lightning Lane ($20–$50/person/day), meals inside parks ($15–$25 per person per meal), and sunscreen (bring your own — bottles cost $15+ inside parks). Resort fees apply at some I-Drive hotels even when not listed upfront.
